_meta.notes: v0.7: Added character fields to quiz cards. Added sentence_build and image_match quiz types. Added image fields to teach cards.

title: Yes, No, Thanks
subtitle: Oo, Hindi, Salamat — Yes, No, and Thank You

cards.body: You already know opo (yes, respectful). Now let's look at the casual version.

example.context: Friend asks if you're coming to the party

cards.body: Remember: oo becomes opo when you add po. Use oo with friends and younger people. Use opo with elders and authority.

example.note: Same word, two registers. Context tells you which one to use.

cards.body: Hindi means 'no.' You already used it in 'hindi po.' On its own, it's casual.

example.context: Friend asks if you want more rice

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Busog' means full (from eating). In Filipino culture, refusing food is a big deal. Saying 'busog na ako' is the polite way to decline without offending the cook.

cards.prompt: Your pare asks 'Want to grab food?' How do you say yes?
cards.explanation: Close friend, same age. 'Oo!' is casual and natural. Opo would be too formal here.

cards.body: For respectful 'no,' add po. You can also soften it with 'pa' (yet).

cards.prompt: Your lola asks if you've finished your homework. You haven't. What do you say?
cards.explanation: 'Hindi pa po' means 'not yet' (respectful). 'Pa' adds 'yet,' which is softer than a flat 'hindi po' (no).

cards.prompt: What's the difference between 'hindi po' and 'hindi pa po'?
options: 'Hindi po' = no. 'Hindi pa po' = not yet.
cards.explanation: 'Hindi po' is a flat no (respectful). 'Hindi pa po' means 'not yet' (respectful). 'Pa' adds the sense of 'yet.'

cards.body: Salamat means 'thank you.' You've already used 'salamat po' in lesson 1.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Salamat' may trace back to Arabic 'salaam' (peace). Ancient trade routes between the Philippines and the Middle East left their mark on the language.

cards.body: Use salamat with friends. Add po for elders and authority.

example.note: Same pattern as everything else. Po is your respect switch.

cards.body: For extra gratitude, say 'maraming salamat.' Marami means 'many' or 'a lot.'
cards.highlight: Maraming salamat = Thank you very much

example.note: Marami + ng = maraming. The 'ng' connects it to salamat.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Marami' means 'many' or 'a lot.' When connecting it to a noun, it becomes 'maraming' (with -ng). You'll see this -ng pattern a lot in Tagalog.

cards.prompt: How do you say 'thank you very much' in Tagalog?
cards.explanation: Maraming salamat = thank you very much. Marami (many) + salamat (thanks).

cards.prompt: Build what you'd say to Lola after she cooks for you:
cards.explanation: 'Maraming salamat po!' Maximum gratitude with respect.

cards.body: In Taglish, mixing 'thank you' and 'salamat' is completely normal. Filipinos switch between them mid-sentence.

example.context: After a coworker helps you with something

cards.prompt: Your boss gives you a compliment. How do you say thank you?
options: Either 'Thank you po' or 'Salamat po' works.
cards.explanation: Both work. 'Thank you po' and 'Salamat po' are both natural with authority. Taglish is normal.

cards.prompt: Build: 'Thank you very much' (respectful)
cards.explanation: 'Maraming salamat po!' Po at the end for respect.

cards.body: When someone says salamat, the natural response is 'walang anuman.'

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Wala' means 'nothing.' 'Anuman' means 'anything.' So 'walang anuman' literally means 'it was nothing.' Same energy as 'no problem' in English.

cards.body: Add po for the respectful version.

example.note: You can also just say 'wala pong anuman' but 'walang anuman po' is more common.

cards.body: In casual speech, Filipinos often skip 'walang anuman' and just say 'okay lang' (it's fine) or 'no problem.'

example.context: Friend thanks you for helping them move

cards.prompt: What does 'walang anuman' mean?
cards.explanation: Walang anuman = You're welcome. Literally 'it was nothing.'

cards.body: Here's a full exchange you might hear at a family gathering.

example.context: Your tita thanks you for bringing dessert

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Anak' means 'child' or 'dear.' Filipino elders use it as a term of endearment for younger people, even if they're not related.

cards.prompt: You're welcome (respectful): '___ anuman po.'
cards.explanation: Walang anuman po. Wala + ng = walang.

cards.prompt: Build the respectful response to 'Salamat!':
cards.explanation: 'Walang anuman po.' It was nothing.

cards.prompt: Your lola says 'Maraming salamat, anak!' What's the best response?
cards.explanation: Lola is an elder. 'Walang anuman po' is the respectful you're welcome.

cards.body: Let's put together everything you've learned. Here's how a full exchange might sound with an elder.

example.context: Your lolo offers you food at a family gathering
example.sentence: "Kumain ka na ba?" "Hindi pa po." "Kain na!" "Salamat po, Lolo."
example.translation: "Have you eaten?" "Not yet." "Eat now!" "Thank you, Grandpa."

cards.body: And here's the same exchange with a friend.

example.sentence: "Kumain ka na ba?" "Hindi pa." "Kain na!" "Salamat, pare!"

cards.prompt: Your tita offers you more food. You're full. Which is the most polite way to decline?
options: Hindi po, busog na po ako.
cards.explanation: 'Hindi po, busog na po ako' means 'No, I'm already full' (respectful). Double po shows extra respect to tita.

cards.body: Saying 'no' to elders often needs softening. 'Hindi po' alone can sound blunt. Adding a reason makes it warmer.

example.sentence: Hindi po, okay lang po. Salamat po.
example.translation: No, it's fine. Thank you. (respectful, warm)

cards.prompt: Someone holds the door for you. Quick, natural response?
cards.explanation: Stranger, quick moment. 'Salamat!' is natural. If they're clearly older, 'salamat po.' No need for maraming salamat for a door hold.

cards.prompt: Your teacher helps you after class. How do you thank them?
options: Either 'Maraming salamat po' or 'Thanks po' works.
cards.explanation: Teacher = authority, and they did something significant. 'Maraming salamat po!' and 'Thanks po' both work. Taglish is natural.

cards.prompt: Your tita thanked you. Build your response:
cards.explanation: 'Walang anuman po.' Po at the end for respect.

cards.prompt: Which of these is a natural response when someone says 'Salamat!'?
cards.explanation: 'Walang anuman!' = You're welcome! The natural response to salamat.

cards.prompt: How do you say 'yes' to a friend?
cards.explanation: Friend = casual. 'Oo!' is the natural yes. Opo is for elders.

cards.prompt: Thank you very much: '___ salamat!'
cards.explanation: Maraming salamat! Marami + ng = maraming.

cards.prompt: What does 'walang anuman' literally mean?
cards.explanation: Wala (nothing) + anuman (anything) = 'it was nothing.' Used as 'you're welcome.'

cards.prompt: Your lolo gives you money for your birthday. How do you thank him?
cards.explanation: Lolo = elder, significant gift. 'Maraming salamat po!' shows maximum gratitude and respect.

cards.prompt: Lolo just gave you a gift. Build your response:
cards.explanation: 'Maraming salamat po!' Po at the end for respect.

cards.prompt: What's the difference between 'hindi po' and 'hindi pa po'?
options: 'Hindi po' = no. 'Hindi pa po' = not yet.
cards.explanation: 'Hindi po' = no (respectful). 'Hindi pa po' = not yet (respectful). 'Pa' adds 'yet.'

cards.prompt: You're welcome (respectful): 'Walang ___ po.'
cards.explanation: Walang anuman po. It was nothing.

cards.prompt: Your mare says 'Salamat!' after you lend her your umbrella. What do you say?
options: Either 'Walang anuman' or 'Okay lang, mare' works.
cards.explanation: Close female friend. Both 'Walang anuman!' and 'Okay lang, mare!' work. No po needed.

cards.prompt: Your tita offers more food. You're full but want to be polite. Best response?
options: Hindi po, busog na po ako. Salamat po.
cards.explanation: Decline with a reason and thank her. Softens the 'no' and shows respect.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete session. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice. E.g., 'User confused hindi po vs hindi pa po (2 wrong attempts). Focus practice on the difference between flat no and not yet.'

alam_mo_ba: 'Salamat' may trace back to Arabic 'salaam' (peace). A reminder of ancient trade routes between the Philippines and the Middle East.
In Filipino culture, refusing food is tricky. The polite move is to accept at least a small portion, say 'busog na po,' then accept more anyway because your tita won't take no for an answer.
'Walang anuman' literally means 'it was nothing,' which has the same energy as the Spanish 'de nada.' Both cultures downplay favours as a form of grace.
Some Filipinos say 'welcome' (English) instead of 'walang anuman.' Taglish strikes again.
The word 'hindi' is also used in compound words. 'Hindi bale' means 'never mind' or 'it doesn't matter.' You'll learn this one soon.
completion_reward.alam_mo_ba_display: Show 1 random fun fact on lesson completion screen. On replay, show a different one.

progress_ring.display: Ring animates from X/5 to (X+1)/5 on session completion
